query: association between digoxin use and prostate cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate-Specific Antigen -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: 5-Alpha reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs) reduce PSA levels by 50%, but this effect typically takes between 6 and 12 months. PSA levels in men on long-term 5-ARI medications should be doubled for screening purposes. Conversely, patients should be screened for prostate cancer before starting 5-ARI medications, specifically due to the delayed effect on PSA levels and the time required for this reduction to become fully apparent. There is no significant change in prostate cancer incidence or progression in men on 5-ARI medications compared to similar male cohorts without the drugs. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Etiology -- Chemical Exposure and Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Veterans exposed to Agent Orange tended to present with prostate cancer at a younger age and higher clinical stage compared to veterans without such exposure. However, overall outcomes were similar. [68] Agent Orange exposure may increase the risk of prostate cancer recurrence, particularly after surgery. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Hormone Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects of hormonal therapy include hot flashes, reduced libido, and loss of bone density resulting in osteopenia or osteoporosis. There are conflicting reports regarding a possible connection between long-term androgen deprivation therapy and cardiovascular risk and metabolic syndrome. Long-term hormonal therapy for prostate cancer increases clotting risk, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, body fat, triglycerides, and insulin resistance while decreasing lean body mass and glucose tolerance. One of the most concerning cardiac effects is the potential to prolong the QTc interval. These effects can be minimized by aggressively treating comorbidities, reducing cardiac risk factors, and eliminating all other drugs that also tend to prolong QTc interval. Urologic medications that typically increase the QTc interval include levofloxacin, amitriptyline, and imipramine. Patients with significant cardiovascular risk factors or pre-existing heart disease are at increased risk and should be monitored closely by cardiology or primary care. Medical check-ups every 3 months have been recommended for this particular high-risk group of patients, especially during the first year of hormonal therapy when the risk of an acute cardiovascular event is the highest. [243] [244]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Prognosis -- Do We Absolutely, Positively Need to Have a Positive Prostate Tissue Biopsy to Treat Prostate Cancer?
retrieved_docs.content: Although a positive tissue biopsy is always preferred before treatment, situations arise where it may not be practical or obtainable. Patients may present requiring treatment who have a history of prostate cancer treated elsewhere with no confirmatory records immediately available. Other patients may have had prior bad experiences with an earlier biopsy and are now refusing all-new diagnostic procedures. Medical conditions such as a recent cardiac stent placement or a history of pulmonary embolisms requiring prolonged anticoagulant therapy may also preclude performing a biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies to Reduce Polypharmacy in Older Adults -- Issues of Concern -- Other Common Pharmaceutical Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacological agents frequently combined with other drugs, potentially causing ADEs, include the following: Long-acting sulfonylureas, which increase the risk of hypoglycemia Metoclopramide, which carries a risk of extrapyramidal adverse effects Testosterone hormone supplements, which elevate the risk of cardiac events Nitrofurantoin, which can lead to lung and liver toxicity Proton pump inhibitors, which are associated with an increased risk of bone fractures, pneumonia, and Clostridium difficile diarrhea. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications, which may cause renal failure, gastrointestinal bleeding, and high blood pressure A thorough review of patient factors and potential drug interactions must be accomplished before combining these agents with other medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Glycoside and Digoxin Toxicity -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Preventative measures for cardiac glycoside and digoxin toxicity include: Avoiding drug interactions Assessing and closely monitoring renal function Monitoring Digoxin levels Measuring electrolyte levels regularly

retrieved_docs.title: Gynecomastia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Underlying medical conditions such as breast cancer, obesity, hypogonadism, adrenal disease, thyroid disease, cirrhosis, renal failure, and malnutrition may contribute to this condition. Tumors of the adrenal glands, pituitary, lungs, and testes can impact hormonal changes resulting in imbalances and ultimately gynecomastia. Medications that have been shown to contribute to this condition include digoxin, thiazides, estrogens, phenothiazines, and theophylline. Use of certain recreational drugs including marijuana has also been associated with this disease. Chemotherapeutic drugs known to cause gynecomastia include methotrexate, alkylating agent, imatinib and vinca alkaloids. The most common drugs, however, have estrogen-like activity and include cimetidine, spironolactone, ketoconazole, and finasteride.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Glycoside and Digoxin Toxicity -- Etiology -- Digoxin Toxicity Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Factors that increase the risk of digoxin toxicity include: Female sex Advanced age Renal insufficiency and end-stage renal disease Hypercalcemia Alkalosis Hypoxemia Acidosis

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Evaluation -- Post-Prostate-Specific Antigen Pre-Biopsy Prostate Cancer Bioassay Risk Stratification Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Several Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved bioassay tests are now available to help risk-stratify patients with persistently elevated levels of PSA up to 10 mg/mL, aiming to reduce unnecessary biopsies in low-risk patients. Both blood and urine sample tests are available. Most tests include different laboratory tests bundled together to provide a single risk score or estimate. Some require a digital prostate massage before specimen collection, and several need significant clinical patient information, which is used in a proprietary algorithm to arrive at their conclusion. Risk stratification bioassay testing is valid, approved, and certified only for PSA levels at or below 10 ng/mL. Patients with a PSA level greater than 10 ng/mL generally proceed directly to a biopsy with or without a preliminary MRI. Bioassay risk stratification testing may be justified in selected patients with PSA levels greater than 10 ng/mL who are otherwise reluctant to undergo a biopsy and require further confirmation of their relative risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Evaluation -- Which Should be Done First: Risk Stratification Bioassays or Prostatic Magnetic Resonance Imaging?
retrieved_docs.content: Rather than exclusively adopting either methodology, a reasonable approach is to perform the MRI initially in higher-risk individuals. However, the bioassay risk stratification test should be started first in lower-risk patients, where a negative finding results in continued observation only.

Therapeutically, several α₁‑adrenergic antagonists—doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesilát, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous—are identified as potential treatments for hypertension, underscoring their role in managing hypertension and its extensive comorbidities. The interrelationships among transient vascular events, chronic cerebrovascular pathology, and prostatic conditions highlight the need for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2956
retrieved_docs.content: UROLOGICALS constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes the specific agents used to treat benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that drugs employed for benign prostatic hypertrophy are a subset of urological medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2456
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY are classified as a subset of the broader ATC category Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that these medications fall within this specific therapeutic group for managing benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC” is classified as a subclass of the broader group of drugs used to treat benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that these agents are part of the therapeutic options for managing this common urological condition within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 580
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم) is clinically linked to a spectrum of genitourinary symptoms, ill‑defined conditions, nausea and vomiting (أعراض الغثيان والقيء), autonomic nervous system disorders (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل), chronic atrial fibrillation (رجفان أذيني مزمن), renal stones (حصاة حالبية), prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا), metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي) and benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد). Management of hypertension often employs alpha‑1 adrenergic blockers—doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesilát, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride and its anhydrous form—agents that are also indicated for treatment of prostatic hyperplasia (تضخم بروستاتي) and benign prostatic hyperplasia, while testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with prostatic hyperplasia. Hyperplasia of the prostate (تضخم بروستاتي) can be treated pharmacologically with terazosin hydrochloride (including the anhydrous form) and surgically with transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP), a procedure that is associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia, general prostate enlargement, and prostate cancer. The intertwined nature of hypertension, its genitourinary and cardiovascular comorbidities, and prostate disorders underscores the importance of an integrated therapeutic strategy that combines appropriate antihypertensive/alpha‑blocker therapy with surgical intervention when indicated to optimize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 735
retrieved_docs.content: UROLOGICALS is a pharmacological class that encompasses drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy, and within this group the category Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC represents a specific subclass of those drugs. This hierarchical relationship clarifies that the ATC‑defined other drugs for benign prostatic hypertrophy are part of the broader urological medication category, aiding clinicians in understanding drug classification and selection for managing benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (ureteral stone), سرطـان البروستاتا النَّقيلي (metastatic prostate cancer), and فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia). This pattern of co‑occurrence indicates that patients who present with nonspecific genitourinary complaints may also have underlying stone disease, advanced prostate malignancy, or benign prostatic enlargement, underscoring the need for thorough diagnostic assessment to identify and manage these potentially related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 227
retrieved_docs.content: palliation. Additional male genital disorders located in the prostate are categorized under prostate disorder and are connected to screening or prophylaxis (التحصّي). This integrated framework illustrates how bladder disorders, prostate disorders, urinary stones, and prostate malignancies interrelate, and how a spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those directed at the prostate—are organized to manage both benign and malignant conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: Doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesylate, prazosin hydrochloride and terazosin hydrochloride are all alpha‑1 adrenergic antagonists that may be used to treat hyperplasia of the prostate (benign prostatic hyperplasia). In contrast, testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, indicating that its use should be avoided in this condition. These relationships highlight the therapeutic role of several α‑blockers in managing prostatic enlargement while underscoring the need to avoid androgenic agents such as testosterone propionate in affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are noted to co‑occur with حصاة حالبية and also with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, indicating that patients who present with vague or nonspecific genitourinary complaints may simultaneously have ureteral stones (حصاة حالبية) or prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing this association can prompt clinicians to consider targeted diagnostic evaluations for these specific urologic pathologies when faced with ambiguous genitourinary presentations, thereby improving detection and management of underlying stone disease or malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: UROLOGICALS is classified within the broader therapeutic domain of GENITO URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES, indicating that urological medications are a subset of agents affecting the genito‑urinary tract and sex hormone pathways. Within the UROLOGICALS category, the specific group of drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy is included, making these agents a further subset of urological drugs. This hierarchical organization clarifies that drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy are a type of UROLOGICALS, and that UROLOGICALS themselves belong to the larger GENITO URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES class, aiding clinicians in understanding the pharmacologic relationships relevant to prostate and related genito‑urinary conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1462
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, because its androgenic activity can exacerbate prostatic enlargement and worsen the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions arise within the urinary system and frequently co‑occur with prostatic disorders, seminal calculi, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer; they are also clinically associated with prostate cancer. The prostate is an organ that belongs to the urinary system and serves as the anatomic site for prostate‑related diseases, including metastatic prostate cancer. Prostatic disorders are situated in the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise located in the prostate, representing subcategories of prostatic disorders. These other male genital disorders are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships emphasize that disturbances of the prostate and associated genitourinary symptoms are central to the presentation, co‑occurrence, and clinical management of prostate disease, particularly cancer, and underscore the importance of appropriate diagnostic screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occur with a spectrum of prostate‑related disorders, including اضطراب بروستاتي (prostatic disorder), حصاة حالبية (ureteral stone), سرطان البروستاتا (prostate cancer), سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي (metastatic prostate cancer), and فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia, BPH). This symptom cluster signals the need to evaluate for malignant disease when nonspecific genitourinary complaints arise. The anatomic site prostata (prostate) is the locus for both benign prostatic hyperplasia (تضخم بروستاتي) and other male genital disorders, and it serves as the target organ for transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP). Clinically, TURP is associated with BPH, providing relief of urinary obstruction and improvement of voiding symptoms, and with prostate cancer, where it may be employed for diagnostic tissue sampling, palliation of obstructive symptoms, or as part of a multimodal therapeutic strategy. Pharmacologic management of BPH commonly involves α‑1 adrenergic antagonists—doxazosin, doxazosin mesylate, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and terazosin anhydrous—which may treat hyperplasia of the prostate. Other male genital disorders classified under اضطراب بروستاتي are linked to routine التحصّي (screening), underscoring the relevance of regular evaluation in men with prostate‑related complaints. Together, the interconnections among genitourinary symptom clusters, prostate anatomy, benign and malignant prostate diseases, surgical (TURP) and medical (α‑1 antagonists) therapies, and screening practices emphasize a comprehensive approach to diagnosis, treatment, and prevention in male urologic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2446
retrieved_docs.content: Finasteride, a 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor, may be used to treat prostate tumor (ورم البروستاتا) by reducing the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, which can help shrink benign prostatic hyperplasia and may also have therapeutic effects on prostatic neoplasms. Its potential benefit in managing prostate tumor highlights the importance of hormonal modulation in urologic oncology and informs clinicians when considering pharmacologic options for patients with prostatic growth abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1885
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions represent a broad clinical category that includes specific bladder disorders (اضطراب في المثانة). These nonspecific genitourinary presentations frequently co‑occur with renal calculi (حصاة حالبية) and are clinically associated with prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). Recognizing that bladder disorders fall under this umbrella, and that patients presenting with such symptoms may also have urinary stones or underlying prostate malignancy, helps clinicians pursue appropriate diagnostic evaluations and tailor management strategies for comprehensive urologic care.
